name: Sai Kung Beach
description: A scenic coastal town on the eastern edge of the New Territories, gateway to the Sai Kung Country Park and the clearest waters in Hong Kong. Take a sampan from the waterfront to reach secluded beaches like Sharp Island and Kiu Tsui.
address: Sai Kung, New Territories, Hong Kong

name: The Chairman
description: Refined Cantonese cooking with a focus on local ingredients and forgotten recipes. The steamed flower crab with aged Shaoxing wine is extraordinary. One of Asia's 50 Best Restaurants. Book weeks in advance.
address: 18 Kau U Fong, Central, Hong Kong

name: Tim Ho Wan
description: The original Michelin-starred dim sum shop that started a global chain. The baked BBQ pork buns are the thing — crisp, sweet, and unlike any other. Come for breakfast or early lunch to avoid the worst of the queue.
address: Shop 72-76, Olympian City 2, 18 Hoi Ting Rd, Mong Kok, Hong Kong

name: Yung Kee Restaurant
description: A Hong Kong legend since 1942. The roast goose — crisp-skinned, fragrant, and served with plum sauce — is the reason to come. The century eggs and congee are equally good. A loud, multi-floor institution in Central.
address: 32-40 Wellington St, Central, Hong Kong

name: Foxglove
description: A hidden speakeasy entered through a vintage umbrella shop in Central. Moody 1960s supper club atmosphere, exceptional cocktails, and a menu of light bites. Dress smartly — the vibe rewards it. One of Asia's 50 Best Bars.
address: 6/F, 6 Duddell St, Central, Hong Kong

name: Victoria Peak
description: The classic Hong Kong panorama — 552 metres above the harbour with one of the most photographed skylines in the world. Take the Peak Tram up for the experience. Best at dusk when the city lights begin to come on.
address: The Peak, Hong Kong

name: Star Ferry
description: The seven-minute harbour crossing between Tsim Sha Tsui and Central has been running since 1888. Still the best HK$3 you'll spend — the view of the skyline from the water is unbeatable, especially at night.
address: Star Ferry Pier, Tsim Sha Tsui, Hong Kong

name: Temple Street Night Market
description: Kowloon's classic night market stretching several blocks in Jordan. Cheap Cantonese food stalls, fortune tellers, mahjong sets, and counterfeit goods alongside the genuine article. Best from 7pm onwards. Walk the whole length before committing to a stall.
address: Temple St, Jordan, Kowloon, Hong Kong

name: Repulse Bay
description: Hong Kong's most popular beach on the south side of Hong Kong Island — wide, calm, and backed by luxury apartment towers. The Repulse Bay Hotel's verandah is ideal for an unhurried lunch or afternoon drinks with a view.
address: Repulse Bay, Southern District, Hong Kong

name: Sheraton Hong Kong
description: The Sheraton Hong Kong Hotel & Towers on Nathan Road, Tsim Sha Tsui — directly on the Kowloon waterfront with harbour views from the upper floors. Well-placed for the Star Ferry, Temple Street, and the TST bar strip.
address: 20 Nathan Rd, Tsim Sha Tsui, Hong Kong

name: Bakehouse
description: A serious Central bakery with some of the best egg tarts in Hong Kong — buttery pastry, silky custard, still warm. The sourdough and croissants draw a morning queue. Small space, fast turnover.
address: G/F, 7 Shing Wong St, Sheung Wan, Hong Kong

name: Bottleman and the Baker
description: A natural wine shop and bakery hybrid in Sheung Wan — pastries, serious sandwiches, and a rotating selection of low-intervention bottles to drink in or take away. The croissant and the wine list are both worth your attention.
address: 55 Bridges St, Sheung Wan, Hong Kong

name: CUÍT Épicerie
description: The grab-and-go outpost of CUÍT in Sai Kung — excellent breads, sandwiches made with real thought, and a small gourmet grocery. A good stop before a beach day in the eastern New Territories.
address: G/F, 25 Chan Man St, Sai Kung, New Territories, Hong Kong

name: Hashtag B
description: A Central bakery with a devoted following for its laminated pastries — the croissants are properly layered and the Danish are worth choosing over the croissant. Small counter, limited quantities daily.
address: G/F, 1 Staunton St, Central, Hong Kong

name: Ki Tsui Cakes
description: A Mong Kok institution for traditional Hong Kong-style cakes and baked goods — pineapple buns, wife cakes, and cocktail buns made the old way. Go in the morning when everything is fresh.
address: G/F, 15 Fong Yiu St, Mong Kok, Kowloon, Hong Kong

name: Levain Bakery
description: A focused Hong Kong bakery producing excellent sourdough and some of the best croissants in Central — properly fermented, well-shaped, and eaten the same morning they come out of the oven.
address: G/F, 36 Cochrane St, Central, Hong Kong

name: Plumcot
description: French-style pastries, croissants, and artisan gelato in a small Central café. The seasonal gelato flavours are creative and well-executed — worth combining with a pastry for a proper mid-morning stop.
address: G/F, 9 Jervois St, Sheung Wan, Hong Kong

name: Social Goods Bakery
description: A minimal Sheung Wan space focused on sandwiches and baked goods — the filling-to-bread ratio is taken seriously and the coffee is better than it needs to be. A reliable working lunch spot.
address: G/F, 48 Cochrane St, Central, Hong Kong

name: Tai Cheong Bakery
description: The most famous egg tart bakery in Hong Kong — the buttery shortcrust pastry shell and barely-set custard filling have been unchanged since 1954. Chris Patten, the last Governor, was a regular. The original Lyndhurst Terrace branch is the one to visit.
address: 35 Lyndhurst Terrace, Central, Hong Kong

name: Tai O Bakery
description: A small village bakery in Tai O serving traditional Lantau-style baked goods to locals and the occasional visitor. The sesame rolls and shrimp paste pastries are made the same way they have been for decades.
address: Tai O Village, Lantau Island, Hong Kong

name: Truffle Bakery
description: A Japanese-style bakery chain that does shokupan — milk bread — better than anyone in Hong Kong. The white Hokkaido loaf is pillowy and slightly sweet. Buy a full loaf and eat half before you leave the shop.
address: G/F, 780 Nathan Rd, Mong Kok, Kowloon, Hong Kong

name: Vission Bakery
description: A takeaway-focused Central bakery known for laminated croissants and sweet pastries — the almond croissant and the kouign-amann sell out early. A quick stop before the mid-morning commute.
address: G/F, 24 Stanley St, Central, Hong Kong

name: Aire
description: A sleek Central cocktail bar with a wine focus and a carefully composed menu of drinks that take the local palate seriously. Low lighting, good music, and a crowd that knows what it ordered.
address: G/F, 50 Staunton St, SoHo, Central, Hong Kong

name: Aqua
description: A TST restaurant and bar on the 17th floor of 1 Peking Road with a panoramic harbour view and an outdoor terrace that is among the best in Kowloon. Italian and Japanese food, excellent cocktails, and a sunset view that justifies the prices.
address: 17/F, 1 Peking Rd, Tsim Sha Tsui, Kowloon, Hong Kong

name: ARGO
description: The Four Seasons Hong Kong's rooftop bar with the most unobstructed view of Victoria Harbour from the Central side — wide harbour panorama, exceptional cocktails, and service that matches the setting. Book a terrace seat before 6pm.
address: 8/F, Four Seasons Hotel, 8 Finance St, Central, Hong Kong

name: Bar Leone
description: A Central cocktail bar by Lorenzo Antinori — one of Asia's 50 Best Bars — doing a focused menu of Italian-inspired drinks with clean technique. The Negroni variations and the tiramisu cocktail are the benchmarks.
address: G/F, The Wellington, 198 Wellington St, Central, Hong Kong

name: Bar.42
description: A Central whisky and cocktail bar with a serious back bar and a low-key atmosphere that encourages staying longer than planned. The staff recommendations are reliable and the selection of Japanese whisky is one of the best in the city.
address: G/F, 42 Cochrane St, Central, Hong Kong

name: BOUND
description: A Sheung Wan cocktail bar with a compact menu and a clear point of view — each drink is built around a single technique or ingredient and executed without compromise. Intimate, serious, and worth the effort to find.
address: G/F, 46 Staunton St, SoHo, Central, Hong Kong

name: Captain's Bar
description: The Mandarin Oriental's classic hotel bar — dark wood, leather, a long bar, and the feeling that serious people have been having serious drinks here since 1963. The Dry Martini is the order and the bar snacks are properly done.
address: Mandarin Oriental Hong Kong, 5 Connaught Rd, Central, Hong Kong

name: Cardinal Point
description: A Central harbour-view bar with resident DJs, an outdoor terrace, and a menu that includes a Pandan Highball worth ordering. The crowd skews young professional and the sound level rises sharply after 9pm.
address: G/F, 8 Wo On Lane, Central, Hong Kong

name: COA
description: Asia's best agave bar and consistently one of the world's 50 best — Jay Khan's SoHo bar has the most serious mezcal and tequila list in Asia and the staff can walk you through every producer on it. The margarita variations are exceptional.
address: G/F, 118-120 Hollywood Rd, SoHo, Central, Hong Kong

name: Cruise Restaurant and Bar
description: A North Point rooftop bar with Asian-inspired cocktails and a relaxed crowd away from the Central tourist circuit. The harbour view from this side of the island is underrated and the prices reflect the less fashionable postcode.
address: Rooftop, 298 King's Rd, North Point, Hong Kong

name: Darkside
description: The Rosewood Hong Kong's dramatic ground-floor bar — double-height ceilings, a 40-metre bar, and a cocktail menu that takes whisky as its organizing principle. The view of Victoria Harbour at night is among the best in Kowloon.
address: Rosewood Hong Kong, 18 Salisbury Rd, Tsim Sha Tsui, Kowloon, Hong Kong

name: Faye
description: A rooftop club in Central with live music and DJ sets — more relaxed than a proper club, louder than a bar. Good for a group that wants to dance without committing to a bottle service situation.
address: Rooftop, 20 Hollywood Rd, Central, Hong Kong

name: Forever Lounge
description: A Hong Kong cocktail bar with a retro-lounge atmosphere and a menu that leans into classic Hong Kong imagery and flavours. The milk tea cocktail and the cha chaan teng-inspired drinks are clever without being precious.
address: G/F, 18 Elgin St, SoHo, Central, Hong Kong

name: Hugger Mugger
description: A small, cosy cocktail bar in SoHo named after the old slang for secrecy — the drinks are creative and the bartenders are happy to make something off-menu if you describe what you want.
address: G/F, 56 Staunton St, SoHo, Central, Hong Kong

name: KABOOM
description: A rooftop bar with a terrace and shisha that draws a lively mixed crowd — cocktails, island-getaway vibes, and a playlist that goes louder as the night goes on. A reliable group destination.
address: Rooftop, 41 Elgin St, SoHo, Central, Hong Kong

name: Ned Kelly's Last Stand
description: A Tsim Sha Tsui institution since 1972 — an Australian-themed bar with live Dixieland jazz every night and a cold beer list that gets the job done. Unpretentious, reliably fun, and exactly what it is.
address: 11A Ashley Rd, Tsim Sha Tsui, Kowloon, Hong Kong

name: Old Man
description: A Central cocktail bar themed around Ernest Hemingway — a focused menu of daiquiri variations and rum-forward drinks in a warm, wood-panelled room. Consistently ranked among Asia's best bars. Order the Daiquiri No. 1.
address: G/F, 37-39 Aberdeen St, SoHo, Central, Hong Kong

name: OZONE
description: The Ritz-Carlton bar on the 118th floor of the ICC tower in West Kowloon — the highest bar in Asia with a view that takes in both sides of the harbour at once. Come for the spectacle and the sunset, stay for the cocktails.
address: 118/F, International Commerce Centre, 1 Austin Rd W, West Kowloon, Hong Kong

name: Peridot
description: The Summit 38 bar at The Henderson — a brand new tower in Central with dramatic interior architecture and harbour views from level 38. The cocktail menu is built around rare spirits and the design rewards looking up.
address: 38/F, The Henderson, 2 Murray Rd, Central, Hong Kong

name: Popinjays
description: The Murray Hotel's wrap-around rooftop bar overlooking Hong Kong Park and the Central skyline — a more civilised alternative to the harbour-view bars, with great sunset drinks and a terrace that works in all seasons.
address: 29/F, The Murray Hong Kong, 22 Cotton Tree Dr, Central, Hong Kong

name: Quinary
description: Antonio Lai's Central bar was one of the pioneers of Hong Kong's cocktail renaissance — multi-sensory drinks, elaborate technique, and a menu that changes with the seasons. The Earl Grey Caviar Martini is the signature.
address: G/F, 56-58 Hollywood Rd, SoHo, Central, Hong Kong

name: Qura
description: A Hong Kong cocktail bar with a Middle Eastern influence — arak-based drinks, spiced syrups, and a food menu that makes a proper evening of it. A different point of view in a city that trends Japanese and European.
address: G/F, 6 Elgin St, SoHo, Central, Hong Kong

name: Soho Corner
description: A European-style corner bar in the heart of SoHo — draught beer, good wine by the glass, and bar food that's actually worth ordering. The terrace is the best spot to watch the Elgin Street crowd flow past.
address: G/F, 1 Elgin St, SoHo, Central, Hong Kong

name: Sunset Grill
description: The Sheraton's terrace bar facing Kai Tak — a plane-spotter's dream and one of the only bars in TST where the view is the runway rather than the harbour. Relaxed, unpretentious, and ideal for an afternoon beer.
address: 18/F, Sheraton Hong Kong Hotel, 20 Nathan Rd, Tsim Sha Tsui, Kowloon, Hong Kong

name: Swim Club
description: The Kimpton Tsim Sha Tsui's pool bar — a rare outdoor pool and bar in Kowloon with harbour views, cocktails, and the kind of hotel bar that attracts non-guests who know about it. Excellent for a weekend afternoon.
address: Kimpton Tsim Sha Tsui Hotel, 28 Minden Ave, Tsim Sha Tsui, Kowloon, Hong Kong

name: The Aubrey
description: The Mandarin Oriental's Japanese cocktail bar — an izakaya concept on the 25th floor with exceptional Japanese whisky, sake cocktails, and small plates. One of the most complete bar experiences in Hong Kong.
address: 25/F, Mandarin Oriental Hong Kong, 5 Connaught Rd, Central, Hong Kong

name: The Iron Fairies
description: A theatrical Central bar designed to look like a Victorian fairy-tale foundry — hammered copper, dim lanterns, and hand-forged details everywhere. The cocktails are good but the design is the real reason to come.
address: G/F, 1 Wyndham St, Central, Hong Kong

name: Wav Rooftop
description: A rooftop bar on H Code in Central with beach-club energy — day beds, cocktails, and a crowd in sunglasses. The best place in Central to feel like you're not in Central.
address: Rooftop, H Code, 45 Pottinger St, Central, Hong Kong

name: Big Wave Bay
description: Hong Kong's best surf beach on the eastern tip of Hong Kong Island — a proper beach with consistent swells, a few surf hire shops, and a beachside bar that makes it easy to spend a full day. Far enough from the city to feel like an escape.
address: Big Wave Bay, Shek O, Southern District, Hong Kong

name: Long Ke Wan
description: One of the most pristine beaches in Hong Kong — a remote Sai Kung beach accessible only by hiking or boat, with turquoise water, white sand, and almost no facilities. The hike in is part of the experience.
address: Long Ke Wan, Sai Kung East Country Park, New Territories, Hong Kong

name: Blue Bottle Coffee
description: The Hong Kong outpost of the San Francisco specialty roaster — familiar, reliable, and a good benchmark for what third-wave coffee looks like in Asia. The pour-overs are well-executed and the space is calm.
address: G/F, H Queen's, 80 Queen's Rd, Central, Hong Kong

name: Cheung Hing Coffee Shop
description: A no-frills cha chaan teng famous for its pineapple buns — the crisp sugar crust on top, the slightly sweet dough, and the wedge of cold butter inside. A Hong Kong breakfast staple done correctly.
address: G/F, 32-34 Lyndhurst Terrace, Central, Hong Kong

name: Cupping Room
description: A Hong Kong specialty coffee roaster with multiple cafés — beans sourced directly from farms across East Africa and Central America, brewed with precision. The baristas can talk about processing methods without being annoying about it.
address: G/F, 1 Lyndhurst Terrace, Central, Hong Kong

name: KLF Coffee
description: A Mong Kok coffee shop with a cult following for its Hong Kong-style milk tea and locally roasted single origins — the Chinese name translates loosely as 'broke person coffee', reflecting the straightforward pricing and zero pretension.
address: G/F, 58 Sai Yeung Choi St S, Mong Kok, Kowloon, Hong Kong

name: Knockbox Coffee Company
description: One of the founding specialty coffee shops of the Hong Kong third-wave scene — the filter coffee programme is serious and the espresso is well-calibrated. A quiet spot to work in SoHo.
address: G/F, 1 Shin Hing St, SoHo, Central, Hong Kong

name: Little Cove Espresso
description: An Australian-style café in Kennedy Town — avocado toast, ricotta hotcakes, and flat whites served in a room that feels transplanted from Sydney. The brunch queue on weekends is part of the neighbourhood ritual.
address: G/F, 42 Davis St, Kennedy Town, Hong Kong

name: NOC Coffee
description: Not Only Coffee — a Hong Kong specialty coffee mini-chain with multiple SoHo locations. Reliable espresso and filter, good pastry selection, and a design sensibility that's clean without being cold.
address: G/F, 10 Shin Hing St, SoHo, Central, Hong Kong

name: The Coffee Academics
description: A Hong Kong specialty coffee group with an educational approach to sourcing and brewing — the seasonal single-origin menu rotates constantly and the staff can explain every step from farm to cup. Good food menu too.
address: G/F, Hysan Place, 500 Hennessy Rd, Causeway Bay, Hong Kong

name: Jia Jia Desserts
description: A Jordan dessert shop that has earned Michelin recognition for its traditional Hong Kong sweet soups — tofu fa, sesame paste, red bean with lotus seed. The kind of place locals bring their parents on Sunday afternoons.
address: G/F, 15 Temple St, Jordan, Kowloon, Hong Kong

name: Mammy Pancake
description: The definitive Hong Kong egg waffle shop — 雞蛋仔, the bubble-studded street snack that has been a Causeway Bay institution since the 1950s. The original flavour in its fresh-made glory is still the reason to queue.
address: G/F, 28 Haven St, Causeway Bay, Hong Kong

name: Snack Baby Gelato
description: A Hong Kong gelato shop pushing genuinely creative flavours alongside the classics — the hojicha, the mango sticky rice, and the rotating seasonal specials are all dense, fresh, and worth the detour.
address: G/F, 5 Gough St, SoHo, Central, Hong Kong

name: Grassy Hill
description: A gentle Tai Po hike with big views over the Plover Cove Reservoir — an easy half-day from the city that rewards with a panorama most visitors never see. Good trail condition year-round.
address: Grassy Hill, Tai Po, New Territories, Hong Kong

name: Lion Rock
description: The iconic silhouette that defines the Kowloon skyline — a 3-4 hour return hike from the Sha Tin Pass with one of the great views in Hong Kong from the rocky summit ridge. The lion's head profile is unmistakable from the city below.
address: Lion Rock Country Park, New Territories, Hong Kong

name: Jardine's Lookout
description: An easy one-hour hike from Causeway Bay to a hilltop lookout with sweeping views over Happy Valley, the harbour, and Kowloon. One of the most accessible serious views in Hong Kong — hard to believe it starts from a residential street.
address: Jardine's Lookout, Eastern District, Hong Kong Island, Hong Kong

name: Tai Mo Shan
description: Hong Kong's highest peak at 957 metres — a proper mountain hike in the New Territories with cloud forest near the summit and views across the entire territory on a clear day. Start early and bring layers.
address: Tai Mo Shan Country Park, New Territories, Hong Kong

name: W Hong Kong
description: The W Hotel in West Kowloon — a design-forward hotel with the WET deck pool bar overlooking the harbour and ICC tower. Non-guests can use the bar and the views justify the prices. A good base for the West Kowloon Cultural District.
address: 1 Austin Rd W, West Kowloon, Kowloon, Hong Kong

name: Lamma Island
description: The most accessible of Hong Kong's outlying islands — a 30-minute ferry from Central to a car-free village with seafood restaurants on the waterfront, hiking trails across the hills, and a pace of life that feels genuinely separate from the city.
address: Lamma Island, Hong Kong

name: Peng Chau
description: The smallest and least-visited of the major outlying islands — a 30-minute ferry from Central to a genuine working-class community with no cars, excellent seafood, and a circuit walk that takes about an hour. Completely overlooked by tourists.
address: Peng Chau, Outlying Islands, Hong Kong

name: Po Toi
description: Hong Kong's southernmost island — accessible by kaito (small ferry) from Aberdeen on weekends, with ancient rock carvings, dramatic cliff walks, and a single seafood restaurant where the whole island eats. A true end-of-the-world feeling.
address: Po Toi Island, Southern District, Hong Kong

name: Flower Market
description: The Prince Edward wholesale and retail flower market — a street of stalls selling cut flowers, potted plants, and orchids at prices far below what you'd pay anywhere else. Best visited early morning when deliveries arrive. Adjacent to the bird market.
address: Flower Market Rd, Prince Edward, Kowloon, Hong Kong

name: Ladies Market
description: Mong Kok's famous stretch of stalls along Tung Choi Street — clothing, accessories, souvenirs, and knock-offs across a dozen blocks. Bargaining is expected and the evening atmosphere is the main event. Come after dark.
address: Tung Choi St, Mong Kok, Kowloon, Hong Kong

name: Sneaker Street
description: Fa Yuen Street in Mong Kok — a block-long concentration of sneaker shops selling every Nike and Jordan release alongside serious vintage pairs. The prices are competitive and the selection is deeper than any single-brand store.
address: Fa Yuen St, Mong Kok, Kowloon, Hong Kong

name: Cat Street
description: Upper Lascar Row in Sheung Wan — an antique and curio market street dealing in Chinese furniture, ceramics, bronzes, and a good proportion of fakes. The fun is in the browsing and the negotiation. Browse the side alleys too.
address: Upper Lascar Row, Sheung Wan, Hong Kong

name: Shanghai Street
description: A Yau Ma Tei street lined with traditional Chinese kitchenware shops — woks, cleavers, bamboo steamers, chopsticks, and clay pots at wholesale prices. A working street that hasn't been touched by gentrification.
address: Shanghai St, Yau Ma Tei, Kowloon, Hong Kong

name: Fruit Market
description: The Yau Ma Tei wholesale fruit market — a working market that has operated on this spot since the 1920s, with traders starting at 2am and the best activity around 5-8am. The yellow tiled buildings are one of the last intact market complexes in Kowloon.
address: Yau Ma Tei Wholesale Fruit Market, Yau Ma Tei, Kowloon, Hong Kong

name: M+ Museum
description: Hong Kong's museum of visual culture in the West Kowloon Cultural District — one of the largest modern and contemporary art museums in Asia, with a permanent collection spanning design, film, and moving image alongside fine art. The building is by Herzog & de Meuron.
address: 38 Museum Dr, West Kowloon Cultural District, Kowloon, Hong Kong

name: Hong Kong Museum of Art
description: Reopened in 2019 after a major renovation, the TST harbourfront museum has the best collection of historical Chinese art and Hong Kong visual heritage in the city — ink paintings, ceramics, and contemporary works across 17,000 pieces.
address: 10 Salisbury Rd, Tsim Sha Tsui, Kowloon, Hong Kong

name: Hong Kong Cultural Centre
description: The harbourfront performing arts complex in TST — controversial for its windowless design that turns its back on one of the world's great views, but the concert hall and opera theatre inside are world-class. Check what's on during your visit.
address: 10 Salisbury Rd, Tsim Sha Tsui, Kowloon, Hong Kong

name: Chi Lin Nunnery & Nan Lian Garden
description: A Tang Dynasty-style Buddhist nunnery and adjacent classical Chinese garden in Diamond Hill — entirely built without a single nail, using traditional mortise and tenon joinery. Free entry to the garden. One of the most serene spots in Kowloon.
address: 60 Fung Tak Rd, Diamond Hill, Kowloon, Hong Kong

name: Choi Hung Estate
description: The most photographed public housing estate in Hong Kong — a crescent of pastel-coloured blocks built in the 1960s surrounding a rooftop basketball court that has become a city icon. Best visited in the morning for the light.
address: Choi Hung Estate, Wong Tai Sin, Kowloon, Hong Kong

name: Happy Valley Racecourse
description: Horse racing on Wednesday nights from September to July — one of Hong Kong's great rituals. The public enclosure costs almost nothing to enter, food stalls and beer are plentiful, and the atmosphere of 30,000 people in a city-centre stadium is something the city does uniquely.
address: 1 Sports Rd, Happy Valley, Wan Chai, Hong Kong

name: Hong Kong Park
description: A large urban park in Central with an aviary, a conservatory, and a visual arts centre inside the historic colonial Flagstaff House. A genuinely quiet green space in the middle of the city's most expensive district.
address: 19 Cotton Tree Dr, Central, Hong Kong

name: K11 Musea
description: A luxury retail and cultural complex on the TST harbourfront that takes its art programming seriously — galleries, installations, and commissioned works throughout the building. The mall surrounds the art rather than the other way around.
address: 18 Salisbury Rd, Tsim Sha Tsui, Kowloon, Hong Kong

name: Kowloon Park
description: A large green space in the heart of Tsim Sha Tsui with a flamingo pond, sculptural garden, and a swimming complex. The best place in Kowloon to sit down after a long walk. The Sunday afternoon tai chi and bird exhibitions are worth timing for.
address: 22 Austin Rd, Tsim Sha Tsui, Kowloon, Hong Kong

name: Kowloon Walled City Park
description: The park on the site of the demolished Walled City — one of the densest human settlements ever recorded, demolished in 1994. The park preserves the original walls and scale, and the small museum documents the remarkable social history of what was there before.
address: Tung Tsing Rd, Kowloon City, Kowloon, Hong Kong

name: Man Mo Temple
description: Hong Kong's most atmospheric temple — a Sheung Wan incense-filled shrine to the gods of literature and war, built in 1847 and unchanged in feel. The coils of incense hanging from the ceiling and the fortune tellers outside are part of the experience.
address: 124-126 Hollywood Rd, Sheung Wan, Hong Kong

name: PMQ
description: The former Police Married Quarters in SoHo, converted into a creative hub for Hong Kong designers and makers — two blocks of 1950s apartments now housing independent studios, design shops, and a good café on the roof. A pleasant afternoon browse.
address: 35 Aberdeen St, SoHo, Central, Hong Kong

name: Stanley Beach & Murray House
description: A south-side Hong Kong day trip — the beach, the waterfront promenade, and the relocated 1844 Murray House now housing restaurants and bars. The Stanley Market behind the main street is good for casual browsing. Easy bus from Admiralty.
address: Stanley, Southern District, Hong Kong

name: Tai Kwun
description: The former Central Police Station compound — a heritage conservation project that has transformed one of Hong Kong's most significant colonial buildings into a centre for arts and culture. The JC Contemporary gallery and the courtyard bar are both excellent.
address: 10 Hollywood Rd, Central, Hong Kong

name: Tian Tan Buddha & Po Lin Monastery
description: The giant bronze Buddha on Lantau — 34 metres high, reached by Ngong Ping cable car or a 7km hike from Tung Chung. The Po Lin Monastery below serves a famous vegetarian lunch. Go on a weekday to avoid the weekend cable car queues.
address: Ngong Ping, Lantau Island, Hong Kong

name: Wong Tai Sin Temple
description: Sik Sik Yuen Wong Tai Sin Temple — one of Hong Kong's most active Taoist temples, receiving hundreds of fortune-seekers daily. The kau cim fortune sticks are shaken in front of the shrine and interpreted by readers who line the surrounding arcade. Best visited on a weekday morning.
address: 2 Chuk Yuen Village, Wong Tai Sin, Kowloon, Hong Kong

name: Victoria Peak Garden
description: The quiet garden above the Peak Tram terminus — a 15-minute walk uphill from the tourist-packed Peak Tower brings you to a tranquil garden with benches and views over the south side of Hong Kong Island. Almost no one makes it up this far.
address: Victoria Peak Garden, The Peak, Hong Kong

name: Yau Ma Tei Tin Hau Temple
description: A 19th-century temple complex in the heart of Yau Ma Tei — five shrines in a courtyard setting that contrasts completely with the surrounding neon and traffic. The adjacent public square is used for Cantonese opera rehearsal on weekday mornings.
address: Market St, Yau Ma Tei, Kowloon, Hong Kong

name: Ando
description: Agustin Balbi's Japanese-Latin American restaurant in Central — one of the most exciting and original kitchens in Hong Kong. The tasting menu crosses culinary traditions in ways that feel genuinely earned rather than arbitrary. Multiple Asia's 50 Best appearances.
address: G/F, Somptueux Central, 52 Wellington St, Central, Hong Kong

name: Australian Dairy Company
description: A Jordan cha chaan teng that has been serving scrambled eggs, milk toast, and macaroni soup to frantic efficiency since 1970. The egg sandwich is the institution's signature — soft scrambled egg between thick toast with a glass of hot milk tea. Expect to share a table.
address: 47-49 Parkes St, Jordan, Kowloon, Hong Kong

name: Caprice
description: The Four Seasons Hong Kong's flagship fine dining restaurant — classic French haute cuisine with a harbour view that makes it one of the most special rooms in Asia. The wine list is serious and the cheese trolley is the best in the city.
address: 6/F, Four Seasons Hotel, 8 Finance St, Central, Hong Kong

name: Canto Spice
description: A modern Cantonese restaurant in Mong Kok doing traditional dishes with current technique — the crispy scallion chicken is the signature and the claypot rice in winter is exceptional. A local favourite away from the tourist circuit.
address: G/F, 8 Argyle St, Mong Kok, Kowloon, Hong Kong

name: Casa Iberica
description: A Spanish grocery and tapas bar in Sheung Wan with the best Ibérico ham in Hong Kong and a wine list focused on natural and traditional Spanish producers. The counter at lunch is one of the better midday options in the neighbourhood.
address: G/F, 71-73 Jervois St, Sheung Wan, Hong Kong

name: Chouchou
description: A Hong Kong bistro doing French classics with real care — beef tartare assembled properly, duck roasted correctly, crème caramel that doesn't wobble. A room of regulars who return for exactly this consistency.
address: G/F, 9 Moon St, Wan Chai, Hong Kong

name: Cristal Room by Anne-Sophie Pic
description: The Hong Kong restaurant from three-Michelin-star chef Anne-Sophie Pic — her most travelled address, bringing the precision of Maison Pic to a dramatic room in Central. The tasting menu changes seasonally and is among the most accomplished French tables in Asia.
address: G/F, The Henderson, 2 Murray Rd, Central, Hong Kong

name: DIECI
description: A ten-dish rotating Italian tasting menu with handmade pasta that changes every month — the concept is simple and the execution is exceptional. One of the most focused Italian restaurants in Hong Kong.
address: G/F, 23 Hollywood Rd, SoHo, Central, Hong Kong

name: Dim Dim Sum
description: A Jordan dim sum restaurant with a playful, modern approach to the format — the har gau and cheung fun are made with precision and the menu extends far beyond the standard trolley selection. Good for a group.
address: G/F, 112 Parkes St, Jordan, Kowloon, Hong Kong

name: Duddell's
description: A Cantonese restaurant in a Central heritage building that doubles as a private art gallery — the walls rotate through significant Chinese contemporary art while the kitchen produces some of the finest dim sum and roast meats in the city.
address: 3/F, Shanghai Tang Mansion, 1 Duddell St, Central, Hong Kong

name: Estro
description: Antimo Maria Merone's Italian restaurant in Central is Hong Kong's finest Italian table — Neapolitan training meets Hong Kong's exceptional produce in a tasting menu that earns its Michelin recognition. The pasta is extraordinary.
address: 29/F, H Code, 45 Pottinger St, Central, Hong Kong

name: Ho Hung Kee
description: A Causeway Bay congee and noodle shop with a Michelin star and no pretension — the wonton noodle soup is a benchmark and the congee variations are among the best in Hong Kong. Queue before 11am.
address: Shop 1045-1046, Hysan Place, 500 Hennessy Rd, Causeway Bay, Hong Kong

name: Holeefook
description: A Central restaurant with a provocative name and serious Cantonese cooking — the roast meats, the stir-fries, and the claypot dishes are executed with care in a room that's always full. Book ahead.
address: 3-5 Elgin St, SoHo, Central, Hong Kong

name: Joy Hing Roasted Meat
description: A Wan Chai roasted meat institution — the char siu is sticky-sweet and properly caramelised, the crispy pork belly has crackling that shatters. A small shop that fills up immediately at lunch. Cash only, fast service.
address: 265 Hennessy Rd, Wan Chai, Hong Kong

name: Kam's Roast Goose
description: A Wan Chai roast goose specialist with a Michelin star — the goose is hung to dry, roasted until the skin is lacquer-crisp, and served the same day. The family has been roasting birds since 1942. Queue by 11:30am or expect a wait.
address: 226 Hennessy Rd, Wan Chai, Hong Kong

name: Kau Kee Restaurant
description: A Central institution since 1920 serving beef brisket noodle soup — the broth is slow-cooked for hours and the brisket falls apart in the bowl. Lunch only, cash only, table sharing obligatory, queue mandatory. Absolutely worth all of it.
address: 21 Gough St, Central, Hong Kong

name: Kung Wo Beancurd Factory
description: A Sham Shui Po tofu factory and shop that has been making beancurd daily since 1893 — the tofu fa (silken tofu dessert) and fried tofu puffs are made fresh every morning and sell out fast. A Kowloon institution that defies gentrification.
address: G/F, 118 Pei Ho St, Sham Shui Po, Kowloon, Hong Kong

name: Kwan Kee Claypot Rice
description: A Sai Ying Pun claypot rice specialist operating from a street stall — the rice is cooked in individual clay pots over charcoal until a crispy crust forms at the bottom, then topped with waxed meats, chicken, or eel. Dinner only, queue from 6pm.
address: 263 Queen's Rd W, Sai Ying Pun, Hong Kong

name: Lan Fong Yuen
description: The cha chaan teng that invented the Hong Kong milk tea — silk-stocking tea, strained through silk stockings for 70 years, poured hot over condensed milk. A Central institution on Gage Street that doubles as a lunch counter for the neighbourhood.
address: 2 Gage St, Central, Hong Kong

name: Louise
description: Julien Royer's Hong Kong restaurant bringing the philosophy of Odette Singapore to a PMQ courtyard setting — French cuisine built around the best local and regional produce, precise without being cold. A serious room for a serious meal.
address: G/F, PMQ, 35 Aberdeen St, SoHo, Central, Hong Kong

name: Luk Yu Tea House
description: The most atmospheric tea house in Hong Kong — 1930s mahogany cubicles, ceiling fans, stained glass, and a cast of regulars who have held the same table for decades. The dim sum is traditional and the experience is irreplaceable. Arrive before 10am.
address: 24-26 Stanley St, Central, Hong Kong

name: Magistracy Dining Room
description: A Cantonese restaurant inside Tai Kwun's heritage police station complex — the setting inside the former Central Magistracy is extraordinary and the food matches it. A special occasion destination with genuine culinary ambition.
address: Tai Kwun, 10 Hollywood Rd, Central, Hong Kong

name: Mak Man Kee Noodle Shop
description: A Jordan noodle shop with a Michelin recommendation for its wonton noodle soup — springy bamboo-pressed noodles, pork and shrimp wontons in a clear broth made from shrimp roe and dried fish. A bowl you'll think about for days.
address: 51 Parkes St, Jordan, Kowloon, Hong Kong

name: Mara
description: A modern Mediterranean restaurant in Sheung Wan with a wood-fired kitchen and a menu that runs from mezze to whole fish. The open kitchen, the natural wine list, and the relaxed room make it one of the best restaurants in its neighbourhood.
address: G/F, 67 Jervois St, Sheung Wan, Hong Kong

name: Migas
description: A Central Mediterranean restaurant, bar, and music lounge that draws a committed crowd for tapas and late-night DJs. The kitchen does Spanish and Moroccan flavours well and the bar stays open longer than most of its neighbours.
address: 8/F, LKF Tower, 33 Wyndham St, LKF, Central, Hong Kong

name: Mono
description: Ricardo Chaneton's Latin American tasting menu restaurant in Central — Venezuelan and French training applied to South American ingredients and techniques. One of the most personal and distinctive tasting menus in Hong Kong.
address: 5/F, 18 On Lan St, Central, Hong Kong

name: Mott 32
description: A large-format Cantonese restaurant in the basement of the Standard Chartered Bank Building — known for its Applewood-smoked Peking Duck, which needs to be ordered 24 hours in advance. The roast meats and dim sum are consistently excellent.
address: Standard Chartered Bank Building, 4-4A Des Voeux Rd, Central, Hong Kong

name: One Dim Sum
description: A Prince Edward dim sum restaurant with a Michelin star and street-level prices — the har gau, siu mai, and egg tarts draw queues every morning from locals who know this is some of the best value dim sum in the city.
address: 15 Playing Field Rd, Prince Edward, Kowloon, Hong Kong

name: Roganic Hong Kong
description: Simon Rogan's Hong Kong outpost of the Cumbrian farm-to-table restaurant — a tasting menu built around exceptional seasonal produce with British sensibility and Asian precision. Consistently Michelin-starred and worth booking well in advance.
address: UG/F, Sino Plaza, 255 Gloucester Rd, Causeway Bay, Hong Kong

name: Tung Po Kitchen
description: A second-floor Wan Chai seafood dai pai dong that became famous partly through Anthony Bourdain — the butter steamed crab, the typhoon shelter prawns, and the crispy toast make an evening that Hong Kong locals consider a rite of passage.
address: 2/F, Konnect, 303 Jaffe Rd, Wan Chai, Hong Kong

name: Yardbird
description: Matt Abergel's yakitori restaurant in SoHo — every part of the chicken grilled over binchotan charcoal, from heart to oyster to tail. One of the foundational restaurants of Hong Kong's modern dining scene and still one of the best reasons to come to this city.
address: 33-35 Bridges St, Sheung Wan, Hong Kong

name: Yat Lok
description: A Central roast goose shop with a Michelin star that operates like a neighbourhood lunch counter — the goose is roasted to order and served over rice in a small, crowded room. Arrive at 11am. Cash only. One of the best simple meals in Hong Kong.
address: G/F, 34-38 Stanley St, Central, Hong Kong

name: Yurt
description: Hong Kong's only modern Central Asian restaurant in SoHo — halal beshbarmaq (hand-pulled noodles with slow-cooked lamb), plov, and fermented mare's milk alongside a considered wine list. An entirely different dining culture presented with genuine care.
address: G/F, 29 Elgin St, SoHo, Central, Hong Kong

name: Zuma
description: The Hong Kong outpost of the international izakaya brand — robata grill, sushi counter, and cocktail bar in a dramatic room in Central. A reliable large-group destination for high-quality Japanese food and drinking without needing to commit to a tasting menu.
address: Level 5 & 6, Landmark Atrium, 15 Queen's Rd, Central, Hong Kong

name: Yuan Heng Spice Company
description: A Sheung Wan spice merchant that has been trading in dried goods, herbs, and Chinese medicine ingredients since the colonial era — rows of barrels containing dried seafood, mushrooms, berries, and roots. The smell alone is worth the walk through.
address: 159 Queen's Rd W, Sheung Wan, Hong Kong
